title: Canto XI
html:
Canto XI

When Bishop Berkeley said ‚Äúthere was no matter,‚Äù858
And proved itÔªø‚Äî‚Äôtwas no matter what he said:
They say his system ‚Äôtis in vain to batter,
Too subtle for the airiest human head;
And yet who can believe it? I would shatter
Gladly all matters down to stone or lead,
Or adamant, to find the World a spirit,
And wear my head, denying that I wear it.

What a sublime discovery ‚Äôtwas to make the
Universe universal egotism,
That all‚Äôs idealÔªø‚Äîall ourselves!Ôªø‚ÄîI‚Äôll stake the
World (be it what you will) that that‚Äôs no schism.
Oh Doubt!Ôªø‚Äîif thou be‚Äôst Doubt, for which some take thee,
But which I doubt extremelyÔªø‚Äîthou sole prism
Of the Truth‚Äôs rays, spoil not my draught of spirit!
Heaven‚Äôs brandy, though our brain can hardly bear it.

For ever and anon comes Indigestion
(Not the most ‚Äúdainty Ariel‚Äù),859 and perplexes
Our soarings with another sort of question:
And that which after all my spirit vexes,
Is, that I find no spot where Man can rest eye on,
Without confusion of the sorts and sexes,
Of Beings, Stars, and this unriddled wonder,
The World, which at the worst‚Äôs a glorious blunderÔªø‚Äî

If it be chanceÔªø‚Äîor, if it be according
To the old text, still better:Ôªø‚Äîlest it should
Turn out so, we‚Äôll say nothing ‚Äôgainst the wording,
As several people think such hazards rude.
They‚Äôre right; our days are too brief for affording
Space to dispute what no one ever could
Decide, and everybody one day will
Know very clearlyÔªø‚Äîor at least lie still.

And therefore will I leave off metaphysical
Discussion, which is neither here nor there:
If I agree that what is, is; then this I call
Being quite perspicuous and extremely fair;
The truth is, I‚Äôve grown lately rather phthisical:860
I don‚Äôt know what the reason isÔªø‚Äîthe air
Perhaps; but as I suffer from the shocks
Of illness, I grow much more orthodox.

The first attack at once proved the Divinity
(But that I never doubted, nor the Devil);
The next, the Virgin‚Äôs mystical virginity;
The third, the usual Origin of Evil;
The fourth at once established the whole Trinity
On so uncontrovertible a level,
That I devoutly wished the three were fourÔªø‚Äî
On purpose to believe so much the more.

To our theme.Ôªø‚ÄîThe man who has stood on the Acropolis,
And looked down over Attica; or he
Who has sailed where picturesque Constantinople is,
Or seen Timbuktu, or hath taken tea
In small-eyed China‚Äôs crockery-ware metropolis,
Or sat amidst the bricks of Nineveh,861
May not think much of London‚Äôs first appearanceÔªø‚Äî
But ask him what he thinks of it a year hence!

Don Juan had got out on Shooter‚Äôs Hill;
Sunset the time, the place the same declivity
Which looks along that vale of Good and Ill
Where London streets ferment in full activity,
While everything around was calm and still,
Except the creak of wheels, which on their pivot he
HeardÔªø‚Äîand that bee-like, bubbling, busy hum
Of cities, that boil over with their scum:Ôªø‚Äî

I say, Don Juan, wrapped in contemplation,
Walked on behind his carriage, o‚Äôer the summit,
And lost in wonder of so great a nation,
Gave way to ‚Äôt, since he could not overcome it.
‚ÄúAnd here,‚Äù he cried, ‚Äúis Freedom‚Äôs chosen station;
Here peals the People‚Äôs voice, nor can entomb it
RacksÔªø‚ÄîprisonsÔªø‚Äîinquisitions; Resurrection
Awaits it, each new meeting or election.

‚ÄúHere are chaste wives, pure lives; here people pay
But what they please; and if that things be dear,
‚ÄôTis only that they love to throw away
Their cash, to show how much they have a-year.
Here laws are all inviolateÔªø‚Äînone lay
Traps for the travellerÔªø‚Äîevery highway‚Äôs clearÔªø‚Äî
Here‚ÄùÔªø‚Äîhe was interrupted by a knife,
WithÔªø‚Äî‚ÄúDamn your eyes! your money or your life!‚ÄùÔªø‚Äî

These free-born sounds proceeded from four pads
In ambush laid, who had perceived him loiter
Behind his carriage; and, like handy lads,
Had seized the lucky hour to reconnoitre,
In which the heedless gentleman who gads
Upon the road, unless he prove a fighter,
May find himself within that isle of riches
Exposed to lose his life as well as breeches.

Juan, who did not understand a word
Of English, save their shibboleth, ‚ÄúGod damn!‚Äù862
And even that he had so rarely heard,
He sometimes thought ‚Äôtwas only their ‚ÄúSalƒÅm,‚Äù
Or ‚ÄúGod be with you!‚ÄùÔªø‚Äîand ‚Äôtis not absurd
To think soÔªø‚Äîfor half English as I am
(To my misfortune), never can I say
I heard them wish ‚ÄúGod with you,‚Äù save that way;Ôªø‚Äî

Juan yet quickly understood their gesture,
And being somewhat choleric and sudden,
Drew forth a pocket pistol from his vesture,
And fired it into one assailant‚Äôs puddingÔªø‚Äî
Who fell, as rolls an ox o‚Äôer in his pasture,
And roared out, as he writhed his native mud in,
Unto his nearest follower or henchman,
‚ÄúOh Jack! I‚Äôm floored by that ‚Äôere bloody Frenchman!‚Äù

On which Jack and his train set off at speed,
And Juan‚Äôs suite, late scattered at a distance,
Came up, all marvelling at such a deed,
And offering, as usual, late assistance.
Juan, who saw the moon‚Äôs late minion863 bleed
As if his veins would pour out his existence,
Stood calling out for bandages and lint,
And wished he had been less hasty with his flint.

‚ÄúPerhaps,‚Äù thought he, ‚Äúit is the country‚Äôs wont
To welcome foreigners in this way: now
I recollect some innkeepers who don‚Äôt
Differ, except in robbing with a bow,
In lieu of a bare blade and brazen frontÔªø‚Äî
But what is to be done? I can‚Äôt allow
The fellow to lie groaning on the road:
So take him upÔªø‚ÄîI‚Äôll help you with the load.‚Äù

But ere they could perform this pious duty,
The dying man cried, ‚ÄúHold! I‚Äôve got my gruel!
Oh! for a glass of max!864 We‚Äôve missed our booty;
Let me die where I am!‚Äù And as the fuel
Of Life shrunk in his heart, and thick and sooty
The drops fell from his death-wound, and he drew ill
His breathÔªø‚Äîhe from his swelling throat untied
A kerchief, crying, ‚ÄúGive Sal that!‚ÄùÔªø‚Äîand died.

The cravat stained with bloody drops fell down
Before Don Juan‚Äôs feet: he could not tell
Exactly why it was before him thrown,
Nor what the meaning of the man‚Äôs farewell.
Poor Tom was once a kiddy upon town,
A thorough varmint, and a real swell,
Full flash,865 all fancy, until fairly diddled,
His pockets first and then his body riddled.

Don Juan, having done the best he could
In all the circumstances of the case,
As soon as ‚ÄúCrowner‚Äôs quest‚Äù866 allowed, pursued
His travels to the capital apace;Ôªø‚Äî
Esteeming it a little hard he should
In twelve hours‚Äô time, and very little space,
Have been obliged to slay a free-born native
In self-defence: this made him meditative.

He from the world had cut off a great man,
Who in his time had made heroic bustle.
Who in a row like Tom could lead the van,
Booze in the ken, or at the spellken hustle?
Who queer a flat?867 Who (spite of Bow-street‚Äôs ban)
On the high toby-spice so flash the muzzle?
Who on a lark with black-eyed Sal (his blowing),
So primeÔªø‚Äîso swellÔªø‚Äîso nuttyÔªø‚Äîand so knowing?868869

But Tom‚Äôs no moreÔªø‚Äîand so no more of Tom.
Heroes must die; and by God‚Äôs blessing ‚Äôtis
Not long before the most of them go home.
Hail! Thamis, hail! Upon thy verge it is
That Juan‚Äôs chariot, rolling like a drum
In thunder, holds the way it can‚Äôt well miss,
Through Kennington and all the other ‚Äútons,‚Äù
Which make us wish ourselves in town at once;Ôªø‚Äî

Through Groves, so called as being void of trees,
(Like lucus from no light); through prospects named
Mount Pleasant, as containing nought to please,
Nor much to climb; through little boxes framed
Of bricks, to let the dust in at your ease,
With ‚ÄúTo be let,‚Äù upon their doors proclaimed;
Through ‚ÄúRows‚Äù most modestly called ‚ÄúParadise,‚Äù870
Which Eve might quit without much sacrifice;Ôªø‚Äî871

Through coaches, drays, choked turnpikes, and a whirl
Of wheels, and roar of voices, and confusion;
Here taverns wooing to a pint of ‚Äúpurl,‚Äù872
There mails fast flying off like a delusion;
There barbers‚Äô blocks with periwigs in curl
In windows; here the lamplighter‚Äôs infusion
Slowly distilled into the glimmering glass
(For in those days we had not got to gasÔªø‚Äî);873874

Through this, and much, and more, is the approach
Of travellers to mighty Babylon:
Whether they come by horse, or chaise, or coach,
With slight exceptions, all the ways seem one.
I could say more, but do not choose to encroach
Upon the Guide-book‚Äôs privilege. The Sun
Had set some time, and night was on the ridge
Of twilight, as the party crossed the bridge.

That‚Äôs rather fine, the gentle sound of ThamisÔªø‚Äî
Who vindicates a moment, too, his streamÔªø‚Äî
Though hardly heard through multifarious ‚Äúdamme‚Äôs:‚Äù
The lamps of Westminster‚Äôs more regular gleam,
The breadth of pavement, and yon shrine where Fame is
A spectral residentÔªø‚Äîwhose pallid beam
In shape of moonshine hovers o‚Äôer the pileÔªø‚Äî
Make this a sacred part of Albion‚Äôs isle.

The Druids‚Äô groves are goneÔªø‚Äîso much the better:
Stonehenge is notÔªø‚Äîbut what the devil is it?Ôªø‚Äî
But Bedlam still exists with its sage fetter,
That madmen may not bite you on a visit;
The Bench too seats or suits full many a debtor;
The Mansion House,875 too (though some people quiz it),
To me appears a stiff yet grand erection;
But then the Abbey‚Äôs worth the whole collection.

The line of lights,876 too, up to Charing Cross,
Pall Mall, and so forth, have a coruscation
Like gold as in comparison to dross,
Matched with the Continent‚Äôs illumination,
Whose cities Night by no means deigns to gloss.
The French were not yet a lamp-lighting nation,
And when they grew soÔªø‚Äîon their new-found lantern,
Instead of wicks, they made a wicked man turn.877

A row of Gentlemen along the streets
Suspended may illuminate mankind,
As also bonfires made of country seats;
But the old way is best for the purblind:
The other looks like phosphorus on sheets,
A sort of ignis fatuus to the mind,
Which, though ‚Äôtis certain to perplex and frighten,
Must burn more mildly ere it can enlighten.

But London‚Äôs so well lit, that if Diogenes
Could recommence to hunt his honest man,
And found him not amidst the various progenies
Of this enormous City‚Äôs spreading span,
‚ÄôTwere not for want of lamps to aid his dodging his
Yet undiscovered treasure. What I can,
I‚Äôve done to find the same throughout Life‚Äôs journey,
But see the World is only one attorney.

Over the stones still rattling, up Pall Mall,
Through crowds and carriages, but waxing thinner
As thundered knockers broke the long sealed spell
Of doors ‚Äôgainst duns, and to an early dinner
Admitted a small party as night fellÔªø‚Äî
Don Juan, our young diplomatic sinner,
Pursued his path, and drove past some hotels,
St.¬ÝJames‚Äôs Palace, and St.¬ÝJames‚Äôs ‚ÄúHells.‚Äù878

They reached the hotel: forth streamed from the front door879
A tide of well-clad waiters, and around
The mob stood, and as usual several score
Of those pedestrian Paphians who abound
In decent London when the daylight‚Äôs o‚Äôer;
Commodious but immoral, they are found
Useful, like Malthus, in promoting marriage.Ôªø‚Äî
But Juan now is stepping from his carriage

Into one of the sweetest of hotels,880881
Especially for foreignersÔªø‚Äîand mostly
For those whom favour or whom Fortune swells,
And cannot find a bill‚Äôs small items costly.
There many an envoy either dwelt or dwells
(The den of many a diplomatic lost lie),
Until to some conspicuous square they pass,
And blazon o‚Äôer the door their names in brass.

Juan, whose was a delicate commission,
Private, though publicly important, bore
No title to point out with due precision
The exact affair on which he was sent o‚Äôer.
‚ÄôTwas merely known, that on a secret mission
A foreigner of rank had graced our shore,
Young, handsome, and accomplished, who was said
(In whispers) to have turned his Sovereign‚Äôs head.

Some rumour also of some strange adventures
Had gone before him, and his wars and loves;
And as romantic heads are pretty painters,
And, above all, an Englishwoman‚Äôs roves882
Into the excursive, breaking the indentures
Of sober reason, wheresoe‚Äôer it moves,
He found himself extremely in the fashion,
Which serves our thinking people for a passion.

I don‚Äôt mean that they are passionless, but quite
The contrary; but then ‚Äôtis in the head;
Yet as the consequences are as bright
As if they acted with the heart instead,
What after all can signify the site
Of ladies‚Äô lucubrations? So they lead
In safety to the place for which you start,
What matters if the road be head or heart?

Juan presented in the proper place,
To proper placemen, every Russ credential;
And was received with all the due grimace
By those who govern in the mood potential,
Who, seeing a handsome stripling with smooth face,
Thought (what in state affairs is most essential),
That they as easily might do the youngster,
As hawks may pounce upon a woodland songster.

They erred, as ag√®d men will do; but by
And by we‚Äôll talk of that; and if we don‚Äôt,
‚ÄôTwill be because our notion is not high
Of politicians and their double front,
Who live by lies, yet dare not boldly lie:Ôªø‚Äî
Now what I love in women is, they won‚Äôt
Or can‚Äôt do otherwise than lieÔªø‚Äîbut do it
So well, the very Truth seems falsehood to it.

And, after all, what is a lie? ‚ÄôTis but
The truth in masquerade; and I defy883
HistoriansÔªø‚ÄîheroesÔªø‚ÄîlawyersÔªø‚Äîpriests, to put
A fact without some leaven of a lie.
The very shadow of true Truth would shut
Up annalsÔªø‚ÄîrevelationsÔªø‚Äîpoesy,
And prophecyÔªø‚Äîexcept it should be dated
Some years before the incidents related.

Praised be all liars and all lies! Who now
Can tax my mild Muse with misanthropy?
She rings the World‚Äôs ‚ÄúTe Deum,‚Äù and her brow
Blushes for those who will not:Ôªø‚Äîbut to sigh
Is idle; let us like most others bow,
Kiss handsÔªø‚ÄîfeetÔªø‚Äîany part of Majesty,
After the good example of ‚ÄúGreen Erin,‚Äù884
Whose shamrock now seems rather worse for wearing.885

Don Juan was presented, and his dress
And mien excited general admirationÔªø‚Äî
I don‚Äôt know which was more admired or less:
One monstrous diamond drew much observation,
Which Catherine in a moment of ‚Äúivresse‚Äù
(In Love or Brandy‚Äôs fervent fermentation),
Bestowed upon him, as the public learned;
And, to say truth, it had been fairly earned.

Besides the ministers and underlings,
Who must be courteous to the accredited
Diplomatists of rather wavering Kings,
Until their royal riddle‚Äôs fully read,
The very clerksÔªø‚Äîthose somewhat dirty springs
Of Office, or the House of Office, fed
By foul corruption into streamsÔªø‚Äîeven they
Were hardly rude enough to earn their pay:

And insolence no doubt is what they are
Employed for, since it is their daily labour,
In the dear offices of Peace or War;
And should you doubt, pray ask of your next neighbour,
When for a passport, or some other bar
To freedom, he applied (a grief and a bore),
If he found not this spawn of tax-born riches,
Like lap-dogs, the least civil sons of bÔªø‚ÄîÔªø‚ÄîÔªøs.

But Juan was received with much ‚Äúempressement:‚ÄùÔªø‚Äî
These phrases of refinement I must borrow
From our next neighbours‚Äô land, where, like a chessman,
There is a move set down for joy or sorrow,
Not only in mere talking, but the press. Man
In Islands is, it seems, downright and thorough,
More than on ContinentsÔªø‚Äîas if the Sea
(See Billingsgate) made even the tongue more free.

And yet the British ‚ÄúDamme‚Äù‚Ää‚Äôs rather Attic,
Your continental oaths are but incontinent,
And turn on things which no aristocratic
Spirit would name, and therefore even I won‚Äôt anent886
This subject quote; as it would be schismatic
In politesse, and have a sound affronting in‚Äôt;Ôªø‚Äî
But ‚ÄúDamme‚Äù‚Ää‚Äôs quite ethereal, though too daringÔªø‚Äî
Platonic blasphemyÔªø‚Äîthe soul of swearing.887

For downright rudeness, ye may stay at home;
For true or false politeness (and scarce that
Now) you may cross the blue deep and white foamÔªø‚Äî
The first the emblem (rarely though) of what
You leave behind, the next of much you come
To meet. However, ‚Äôtis no time to chat
On general topics: poems must confine
Themselves to unity, like this of mine.888

In the great worldÔªø‚Äîwhich, being interpreted,
Meaneth the West or worst end of a city,
And about twice two thousand people bred
By no means to be very wise or witty,
But to sit up while others lie in bed,
And look down on the Universe with pityÔªø‚Äî
Juan, as an inveterate patrician,
Was well received by persons of condition.

He was a bachelor, which is a matter
Of import both to virgin and to bride,
The former‚Äôs hymeneal hopes to flatter;
And (should she not hold fast by Love or Pride)
‚ÄôTis also of some moment to the latter:
A rib‚Äôs a thorn in a wed gallant‚Äôs side,
Requires decorum, and is apt to double
The horrid sinÔªø‚Äîand what‚Äôs still worse, the trouble.

But Juan was a bachelorÔªø‚Äîof arts,
And parts, and hearts: he danced and sung, and had
An air as sentimental as Mozart‚Äôs
Softest of melodies; and could be sad
Or cheerful, without any ‚Äúflaws or starts,‚Äù889
Just at the proper time: and though a lad,
Had seen the worldÔªø‚Äîwhich is a curious sight,
And very much unlike what people write.

Fair virgins blushed upon him; wedded dames
Bloomed also in less transitory hues;890
For both commodities dwell by the Thames,
The painting and the painted; Youth, Ceruse,891
Against his heart preferred their usual claims,
Such as no gentleman can quite refuse:
Daughters admired his dress, and pious mothers
Inquired his income, and if he had brothers.

The milliners who furnish ‚Äúdrapery Misses‚Äù892
Throughout the season, upon speculation
Of payment ere the Honeymoon‚Äôs last kisses
Have waned into a crescent‚Äôs coruscation,
Thought such an opportunity as this is,
Of a rich foreigner‚Äôs initiation,
Not to be overlookedÔªø‚Äîand gave such credit,
That future bridegrooms swore, and sighed, and paid it.

The Blues, that tender tribe, who sigh o‚Äôer sonnets,
And with the pages of the last Review
Line the interior of their heads or bonnets,
Advanced in all their azure‚Äôs highest hue:
They talked bad French or Spanish, and upon its
Late authors asked him for a hint or two;
And which was softest, Russian or Castilian?
And whether in his travels he saw Ilion?

Juan, who was a little superficial,
And not in literature a great Drawcansir,893
Examined by this learn√®d and especial
Jury of matrons, scarce knew what to answer:
His duties warlike, loving or official,
His steady application as a dancer,
Had kept him from the brink of Hippocrene,
Which now he found was blue instead of green.

However, he replied at hazard, with
A modest confidence and calm assurance,
Which lent his learn√®d lucubrations pith,
And passed for arguments of good endurance.
That prodigy, Miss Araminta Smith
(Who at sixteen translated ‚ÄúHercules Furens‚Äù
Into as furious English), with her best look,
Set down his sayings in her common-place book.

Juan knew several languagesÔªø‚Äîas well
He mightÔªø‚Äîand brought them up with skill, in time
To save his fame with each accomplished belle,
Who still regretted that he did not rhyme.
There wanted but this requisite to swell
His qualities (with them) into sublime:
Lady Fitz-Frisky, and Miss Maevia Mannish,
Both longed extremely to be sung in Spanish.

However, he did pretty well, and was
Admitted as an aspirant to all
The coteries, and, as in Banquo‚Äôs glass,
At great assemblies or in parties small,
He saw ten thousand living authors pass,
That being about their average numeral;
Also the eighty ‚Äúgreatest living poets,‚Äù894
As every paltry magazine can show it‚Äôs.

In twice five years the ‚Äúgreatest living poet,‚Äù
Like to the champion in the fisty ring,
Is called on to support his claim, or show it,
Although ‚Äôtis an imaginary thing.
Even IÔªø‚Äîalbeit I‚Äôm sure I did not know it,
Nor sought of foolscap subjects to be kingÔªø‚Äî
Was reckoned, a considerable time,
The grand Napoleon of the realms of rhyme.895

But Juan was my Moscow, and Faliero
My Leipzig, and my Mont Saint Jean seems Cain:896
La Belle Alliance of dunces down at zero,
Now that the Lion‚Äôs fallen, may rise again:
But I will fall at least as fell my Hero;
Nor reign at all, or as a monarch reign;
Or to some lonely isle of gaolers go,
With turncoat Southey for my turnkey Lowe.897

Sir Walter reigned before me; Moore and Campbell
Before and after; but now grown more holy,
The Muses upon Zion‚Äôs hill must ramble
With poets almost clergymen, or wholly;
And Pegasus has a psalmodic amble
Beneath the very Reverend Rowley Powley,898899
Who shoes the glorious animal with stilts,
A modern Ancient PistolÔªø‚Äî‚Äúby these hilts!‚Äù900

Still he excels that artificial hard
Labourer in the same vineyard, though the vine
Yields him but vinegar for his reward.Ôªø‚Äî
That neutralised dull Dorus of the Nine;
That swarthy Sporus, neither man nor bard;
That ox of verse, who ploughs for every line:Ôªø‚Äî
Cambyses‚Äô roaring Romans beat at least
The howling Hebrews of Cybele‚Äôs priest.Ôªø‚Äî901

Then there‚Äôs my gentle EuphuesÔªø‚Äîwho, they say,902
Sets up for being a sort of moral me;903
He‚Äôll find it rather difficult some day
To turn out both, or either, it may be.
Some persons think that Coleridge hath the sway;
And Wordsworth has supporters, two or three;
And that deep-mouthed Boeotian ‚ÄúSavage Landor‚Äù904
Has taken for a swan rogue Southey‚Äôs gander.

John Keats, who was killed off by one critique,
Just as he really promised something great,
If not intelligible, without Greek
Contrived to talk about the gods of late,
Much as they might have been supposed to speak.905
Poor fellow! His was an untoward fate;
‚ÄôTis strange the mind, that very fiery particle,906907
Should let itself be snuffed out by an article.

The list grows long of live and dead pretenders
To that which none will gainÔªø‚Äîor none will know
The conqueror at least; who, ere Time renders
His last award, will have the long grass grow
Above his burnt-out brain, and sapless cinders.
If I might augur, I should rate but low
Their chances;Ôªø‚Äîthey‚Äôre too numerous, like the thirty908
Mock tyrants, when Rome‚Äôs annals waxed but dirty.

This is the literary lower empire,
Where the praetorian bands take up the matter;Ôªø‚Äî
A ‚Äúdreadful trade,‚Äù like his who ‚Äúgathers samphire,‚Äù909
The insolent soldiery to soothe and flatter,
With the same feelings as you‚Äôd coax a vampire.
Now, were I once at home, and in good satire,
I‚Äôd try conclusions with those Janizaries,
And show them what an intellectual war is.

I think I know a trick or two, would turn
Their flanks;Ôªø‚Äîbut it is hardly worth my while,
With such small gear to give myself concern:
Indeed I‚Äôve not the necessary bile;
My natural temper‚Äôs really aught but stern,
And even my Muse‚Äôs worst reproof‚Äôs a smile;
And then she drops a brief and modern curtsy,
And glides away, assured she never hurts ye.

My Juan, whom I left in deadly peril
Amongst live poets and blue ladies, passed
With some small profit through that field so sterile,
Being tired in timeÔªø‚Äîand, neither least nor last,
Left it before he had been treated very ill;
And henceforth found himself more gaily classed
Amongst the higher spirits of the day,
The Sun‚Äôs true son, no vapour, but a ray.

His morns he passed in businessÔªø‚Äîwhich dissected,
Was, like all business, a laborious nothing
That leads to lassitude, the most infected
And Centaur Nessus garb of mortal clothing,910
And on our sofas makes us lie dejected,
And talk in tender horrors of our loathing
All kinds of toil, save for our country‚Äôs goodÔªø‚Äî
Which grows no better, though ‚Äôtis time it should.

His afternoons he passed in visits, luncheons,
Lounging and boxing; and the twilight hour
